perturbation.original_text: 7.2 Each Party agrees to negotiate in good faith the terms of the Manufacturing Agreement and to use its reasonable endeavours to enter into said agreement within 1 DAY DN 20/5/2010 of the Commencement Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2 Each Party agrees to negotiate in good faith the terms of the Manufacturing Agreement and to use its reasonable endeavours to enter into said agreement within 365 DAYS of the Commencement Date.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract stipulates that the parties must enter into a Manufacturing Agreement within 1 day of the commencement date. Changing this to 365 days introduces a significant delay that could be considered commercially unreasonable and could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is a principle of contract law under English law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Various case law precedents in English contract law
law_url1: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 implies some duties.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a codified statute, the implied duty of good faith is a core principle in English contract law. A delay of 365 days to finalize a critical manufacturing agreement could be seen as a failure to act in good faith, potentially hindering the objectives of the agreement. Though there is no exact codified law, multiple legal cases outline this duty. While the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 says that a person who is not a party to a contract has no right to enforce it, it does imply some rights.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a very short timeframe (1 day) for entering into a Manufacturing Agreement. Changing this to 365 days introduces a substantial delay that the Perturbation Explanation states could be considered commercially unreasonable and could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a delay of 365 days to finalize a critical manufacturing agreement could be seen as a failure to act in good faith. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition supports assigning YES, as the altered deadline violates the implied duty of good faith, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 offers no relevant information, the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation sufficiently point to a contradiction of the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 8. NON-SOLICITATION Each Party agrees that during the Term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months thereafter it shall not, without the prior written consent of the other Party, either on its own account or through its employees or agents or otherwise or on behalf of any other person, firm, company or other organisation and other than by general advertising, solicit, interfere with, procure or entice away (or, in each case, attempt so to do), either directly or indirectly, any employee or contractor of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. NON-SOLICITATION Each Party agrees that during the Term of this Agreement and for a period of twenty-four (24) months thereafter it shall not, without the prior written consent of the other Party, either on its own account or through its employees or agents or otherwise or on behalf of any other person, firm, company or other organisation and other than by general advertising, solicit, interfere with, procure or entice away (or, in each case, attempt so to do), either directly or indirectly, any employee or contractor of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-solicitation period to 24 months may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under English law, particularly if it hinders an individual's ability to find employment. Courts in England carefully scrutinize non-solicitation clauses for reasonableness in duration and scope.
perturbation.law_explanation: English law views non-solicitation clauses as restraints of trade. For a clause to be enforceable, it must be reasonable in both duration and scope to protect legitimate business interests. A 24-month non-solicitation period may be considered excessive, particularly if it unduly restricts an individual's ability to seek employment or a company's ability to fairly compete. The case of Bridge v Deacons provides judicial guidance on the enforceability of restraint of trade clauses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: case | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you case A case is a civil or criminal proceeding at law or in equity . It can also be referred to as an action , suit , or controversy , depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the dispute . A civil case normally involves two parties with equal status, such as a case between two citizens or a case between a citizen and a corporation . A criminal case typically involves two parties with different statuses; one party (the prosecutor ) represents the State - the other party (the defendant ) generally speaking, is an individual or a corporation. See controversy for an explanation of the “ case or controversy ” requirement in federal court . [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS appellate procedure class actions courts criminal procedure wex definitions civil procedure constitutional law criminal law and procedure government legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to 24 months, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, may be an unreasonable restraint of trade under English law. Although the scraped snippets don't directly address the length of non-solicitation clauses, the Law Explanation, which is informed by legal principles, states that such clauses must be reasonable in duration and scope. The change introduces a potential violation by excessively restricting an individual's ability to seek employment, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined under inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: 20.4 Any Notice shall be deemed to have been served: 20.4.1 if delivered by hand, at the time and date of delivery; 20.4.2 if sent by fax, at the time and date of the successful fax transmission report; 20.4.3 if sent by recorded delivery or registered post, 48 hours from the date of posting (such date as evidenced by postal receipt etc); or 20.4.4 if sent by registered airmail, five days from the date of posting.
perturbation.changed_text: 20.4 Any Notice shall be deemed to have been served: 20.4.1 if delivered by hand, ten days after the time and date of delivery; 20.4.2 if sent by fax, ten days after the time and date of the successful fax transmission report; 20.4.3 if sent by recorded delivery or registered post, thirty days from the date of posting (such date as evidenced by postal receipt etc); or 20.4.4 if sent by registered airmail, sixty days from the date of posting.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deemed service times to be drastically longer, particularly for recorded delivery/registered post and registered airmail (30 and 60 days respectively), could contravene principles of fairness and reasonableness in contractual notices. This would be relevant if a notice was time-sensitive, e.g. triggering a right to terminate the contract for breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Reasonable Notice Principles in Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Mannai Investment Co Ltd v Eagle Star Life Assurance Co Ltd [1997] AC 749
perturbation.law_explanation: While no statute dictates the precise timing for deemed service of notices, English courts require notices to be clear, unambiguous, and served within a reasonable timeframe. The Mannai Investment Co Ltd v Eagle Star Life Assurance Co Ltd case emphasizes the importance of clarity and the recipient's ability to understand the notice. Delaying deemed service by 30-60 days may prevent a party from acting on critical information within the contractually intended timeframe, potentially leading to unfair or commercially unreasonable outcomes. The principle is based on the construction of the contract as a whole and what a reasonable person would understand the terms to mean.
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text introduces significant delays in the deemed service of notices, potentially leading to unfair or commercially unreasonable outcomes. The Law Explanation suggests that while no statute dictates the exact timing, courts require notices to be served within a reasonable timeframe. The added delays of 30-60 days for registered mail and airmail could prevent a party from acting on critical information within the contractually intended timeframe. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.
